steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures being joined
steps.content: Two independent clauses need connection: “Compiling isn't exclusive to certain programming languages” and “any language can incorporate this tool.” They express related ideas with a contrasting relationship—compiling isn't limited to some languages; it's available to all languages.

steps.title: Apply the rule for conjunctive adverbs between independent clauses
steps.content: The word “however” is a conjunctive adverb that must join these two independent clauses. The standard punctuation rule requires a semicolon before the conjunctive adverb and a comma after: IC; however, IC. This pattern maintains both clauses as complete thoughts while showing their logical relationship. Option A follows this pattern exactly. Option B uses only commas (creating a comma splice), Option C breaks the clauses into two separate sentences, and Option D places the semicolon after “however” instead of before it, violating the rule. That matches choice A — A is correct.
